author: taulepton_
content: I agree that we should not consider zkBoost or ere as the only implementations capable of verifying or generating proofs. For verification, we planned to have a binary with various bindings that we can import directly into the CL (similar to kzg verification). There would be no requirement for an external proof node unless you wanted to generate proofs. We have created an `execution-proofs-APIs` repo that defines the specification (not yet landed) for the proof node interface. `zkboost` is a reference implementation of this spec, but we encourage others to be developed.

author: barnabasbusa
content: curiuos about how client teams wanna process these proofs too

author: barnabasbusa
content: I still think having to run the EL side by side will reduce the 'pressure' on CL teams to add verification logic native to their client

author: barnabasbusa
content: as everyone just gonna say, whats the benefit?

author: jsign
content: You mean in the stage of "optional" proofs instead of "mandatory" ones?

author: jsign
content:
I think the main argument for us proposing 8025, is that it helps a lot many actors gain experience in a big architectural change for Ethereum while trying to minimize risk as much as possible (i.e. 8025 is designed so that proof validation is not consensus-critical at this stage).

Still asking people to run the EL is related to minimizing risk, and is not tied to the final value trying to be delivered (i.e. mandatory proofs).

I would actually like to be very clear that we do not want the whole network to opt in here, so actually see these recommendations of still recommending running ELs as the right friction to keep the risk low. If everyone YOLOs on being a zkvalidator, we would be putting the network at risk.

I also think there are other "reality challenges" of this not being "formalized" in scheduled EIP. For example, for the execution-specs for the guest program and execution witness generation, I had to add many new features to the testing framework and tooling. Unless EIPs are proposed, is quite hard to justify teams allocating some resources to start making necessary changes of these with time and keep things maintainable with al lthe rest of things that are happening. Same with EL and CL changes.

I think this eip mentioning something optional is an exception mostly related to the ambitious proposal of this change.

So i agree with you this "optional thing" can have some effects of CLs not being motivated to go all in into what we would desired immediately, but maybe that can be seen as a feature and not a bug (although can feel somewhat contradictory).

(Sorry for my long messages -- i tend to write too much sometimes trying to be clear, which might be annoying 😅 )
